region_of_origin: China; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for tea leaf egg; mapped to the closest parent class 'hen egg food product' (FOODON_03302184). Tea leaf egg is a prepared, marinated egg product.
ontology.foodex2_description: Eggs from hens — cooked/processed egg product (closest FoodEx2 anchor for a marinated hard-boiled egg preparation)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tea leaf egg. A00LC (hen eggs, processed) is the closest defensible anchor. Marination with tea, soy sauce and spices is not captured in the code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for tea leaf egg or marinated egg preparation as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one medium tea leaf egg weighing approximately 55 g, providing around 72 kcal and 7.4 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Tea Leaf Egg — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists tea leaf egg (茶叶蛋) as a named entry with per-100g values matching the input record exactly. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate), sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from this HPB entry. Saturated fat (2.5 g), monounsaturated fat (3.2 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g), trans fat (0.0 g), potassium (126 mg), and cholesterol (373 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central hard-boiled egg entry (NDB 01129 / FDC ID 172185) as HPB Foodo does not report full fatty acid profile, potassium, or cholesterol for this item. This adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Foodo
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tea leaf egg contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Foodo. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate content; foods with negligible carbohydrate are classified as not applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI value is assigned.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (849 mg/100 g) from soy sauce and salt brine
hypertension.guidance: Tea leaf egg contains 849 mg sodium per 100 g, well above the 600 mg/100 g threshold; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to occasional small servings and consider preparing with low-sodium soy sauce to reduce the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tea leaf egg contains 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g with 13.4 g protein, making it an excellent low-glycaemic protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high cholesterol content (~373 mg/100 g) warrants moderation in those with co-existing dyslipidaemia, and the high sodium (849 mg/100 g) requires attention given the frequent co-existence of hypertension in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (849 mg/100 g) and moderate cholesterol (~373 mg/100 g); saturated fat is moderate at ~2.5 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (849 mg/100 g) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, and the cholesterol content (~373 mg/100 g) warrants moderation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit tea leaf egg to one egg per occasion and favour home preparation with low-sodium soy sauce to reduce overall sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, star anise) are beneficial; high sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The braising spices (cinnamon and star anise) provide polyphenol and anti-inflammatory benefit, but the high sodium content (849 mg/100 g) may exacerbate RA-related oedema; limit to one egg per occasion and reduce soy sauce when preparing at home.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tea leaf egg (茶叶蛋, chá yè dàn) is a traditional Chinese and Singaporean snack or breakfast item consisting of a hard-boiled hen egg whose shell is gently cracked after the initial boil and then simmered for an extended period in a fragrant braising liquid made from black tea leaves, soy sauce, star anise, cinnamon stick, salt, and five-spice powder. The cracked shell creates a distinctive marbled pattern on the egg white as the dark brine seeps through the fissures. The resulting egg has a firm, slightly rubbery white with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour and a fully set yolk. Tea leaf eggs are sold widely at convenience stores, hawker centres, and roadside stalls across Singapore, Taiwan, China, and the broader Chinese diaspora. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 131 kcal, 13.4 g protein, 8.6 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. The sodium content is high at 849 mg/100 g due to the soy sauce and salt brine, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The egg contributes meaningful calcium (182 mg/100 g) and iron (1.3 mg/100 g). Glycemic index is not applicable given the negligible carbohydrate content.

confidence_reason: Core macros, sodium, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which lists tea leaf egg as a named entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central hard-boiled egg data (NDB 01129 / FDC ID 172185) as HPB does not report full fatty acid profile, potassium, or cholesterol for this item; these are documented as estimated values in notes.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — tea leaf egg contains no starch or sugar ingredients in material quantity; the braising liquid contributes negligible carbohydrate to the egg itself. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as eggs contain no dietary fibre. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for a hen egg product per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 172185). Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g/100 g), potassium_mg (126.0 mg/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (373.0 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central hard-boiled egg (NDB 01129 / FDC ID 172185) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid fractions, potassium, or cholesterol for this item. vitamin_c_mg set to -1: HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central do not report vitamin C for this specific marinated egg preparation; tea braising liquid may contribute trace amounts but no reliable figure is available. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: detailed vitamin A data for tea leaf egg as a marinated preparation are not individually reported in HPB Foodo; follow-up proximate analysis recommended. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; USDA hard-boiled egg reference does not account for sodium-brine marination effects on B-vitamin content. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_b6_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_b12_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. phosphorus_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. magnesium_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. zinc_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. water_g set to -1: moisture content for tea leaf egg as a marinated preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo; the extended simmering and brine absorption alter moisture relative to a plain hard-boiled egg. All -1 fields reflect genuine data gaps in HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central for this specific marinated preparation as of 2026-04-16; a full proximate analysis of the commercial product is recommended to resolve these gaps. Glycemic index is not applicable: tea leaf egg is a negligible-carbohydrate food (0 g/100 g per HPB Foodo); GI measurement is not meaningful for this food type. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the input record is incorrect and has been removed — tea leaf egg is an animal-derived product (hen egg). Halal status is assessed as likely halal (egg, tea, spices) but formal certification should be verified for commercial products.
